data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: AMAT, RUM, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged up in premarket trading on Friday after Wall Street set record highs, propelled by strong performances in technology and semiconductor stocks, even as market participants navigated the challenges of a government shutdown and the postponement of critical payroll data.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Rumble (NASDAQ:RUM) +12% - Shares jumped after the company announced a partnership with AI answer engine Perplexity to integrate AI-powered tools and boost discoverability on its video platform. The deal includes a new bundled subscription combining both companies’ premium plans, while Perplexity’s new product Comet will be promoted across the Rumble ecosystem, including the Rumble Advertising Center. The integration of Perplexity’s search technology into Rumble.com will roll out progressively, though financial terms were undisclosed.
  • USA Rare Earth (NASDAQ:USAR) +7% - Shares soared after CEO Barbara Humpton stated on CNBC that the company is in close communication with President Trump's administration. The news, which followed previous government investments in other critical metals suppliers like MP Materials (MP) and Lithium Americas (LAC), fueled speculation of a potential federal stake in the company. Humpton commented that it will take many players to build out the U.S. rare earth supply chain and that the company is keeping the administration informed of its plans, adding to investor optimism.

Biggest stock losers

  • Applied Materials (NASDAQ:AMAT) -3% -Shares slipped after the chip equipment maker warned that a new U.S. export rule will further limit its sales to China. The company said that the updated “BIS Affiliates Rule” could cut its fourth-quarter revenue by approximately $110M and its FY2026 revenue by around $600M. This new restriction adds to the pressure on the company, which has already trailed its peers this year due to its significant exposure to the Chinese market.

data.title: UK service sector growth eases to five-month low
data.description:
• S&P Global UK Services PMI At 50.8 in September, easing considerably from August's 16-month high of 54.2. The headline index has posted above the neutral 50.0 threshold in each month sinceMay, but the latest reading was the weakest over this period and indicated only a modest rise in activity.
  • S&P Global UK Composite PMI at 50.1, down from 53.5 in August. 
  • "UK service providers experienced a disappointing end to the third quarter as weak consumer confidence, delays to business spending decisions and falling exports all weighed on demand. Business activity expansion hit a five-month low, while new order gains were much softer than the 11-month high seen in August." said Tim Moore, Economics Director at S&P Global Market Intelligence.
  • ETFs: (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB)
  • Currency: (GBP:USD)

data.title: Indonesia suspends TikTok's license over data-sharing issue
data.description:
Indonesia has temporarily suspended ByteDance (BDNCE)-owned TikTok’s local operating license after the company refused to fully share data on its live streaming activity during countrywide protests in August.

"This step is a form of government assertiveness after TikTok only provided partial data on TikTok Live activity during the demonstration period of August 25–30, 2025," said Director General of Digital Space Supervision, Alexander Sabar, according to a Google-translated press release from Indonesia's Ministry of Communication and Digital (Komdigi). 

ByteDance did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

The app remains accessible in Indonesia for now, Bloomberg News reported. TikTok had paused its live streaming feature on Aug. 30 as protests grew in the country but resumed the feature a few days later after tensions eased.

Sabar noted that due to the alleged monetization of live activity from accounts suspected of online gambling activities, the ministry submitted a request that includes traffic information, live streaming activity, and monetization data, including the number and value of gifts given. 

Sabar added that they summoned TikTok to provide clarification in person on Sept. 16, and TikTok was given until Sept. 23 to submit the requested data.

TikTok responded in a letter dated Sept. 23 that it has internal policies and procedures that regulate how to handle and respond to data requests, so it could not provide the requested data, according to the statement.

"Therefore, Komdigi assesses that TikTok has violated its obligations as a Private ESP [Electronic System Provider], and we have taken the step of temporarily freezing the TDPSE [Electronic System Provider Registration Certificate] as a form of follow-up supervision," said Sabar.

data.title: Japan's ruling party prepares to elect new leader on October 4
data.description:
The Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) is set to choose a new president on Saturday, a decision that typically determines Japan’s next Prime Minister and guide fiscal and central bank policies.

The Liberal Democratic Party will elect a successor to former Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba, who resigned last month, with debate centered on household relief versus fiscal restraint.

Among the five competitors, the leading candidates are Sanae Takaichi, who could become Japan's first female prime minister, Shinjiro Koizumi, a reform-minded young contender, and Yoshimasa Hayashi, a seasoned moderate.

Takaichi, a conservative nationalist, is advocating for a program of bold fiscal stimulus and massive state investment in technology, infrastructure, and food security, with the ambitious goal of doubling the Japanese economy within ten years. She has also suggested reviewing the U.S.-Japan trade deal.

Conversely, Koizumi, along with candidates like Yoshimasa Hayashi, favors a more moderate approach, supporting household tax relief while largely maintaining Ishiba's cautious economic policies.

The incoming leader will immediately deal with internal party division, slow growth, and the upcoming high-profile visit of U.S. President Trump in October.

Separately, Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda reaffirmed that rates will rise if economic and price trends align with forecasts. Ueda noted that US tariffs are squeezing exporters’ profits, particularly in autos, though broader impacts on investment, employment, and wages remain limited.

Meanwhile, data showed Japan’s unemployment rate climbed to 2.6% in August, the highest in 13 months and above expectations of 2.4%.

The Nikkei 225 Index jumped 1.85% to close at 45,769, while the broader Topix rose 1.35% to 3,129 on Friday, with the Nikkei finishing at a fresh record as enthusiasm for artificial intelligence drove Japanese chip stocks higher. The Japanese yen slipped to around 147.5 per dollar on Friday, retreating from two-week highs.

More on Japan

  • Japan retail sales and industrial output unexpectedly fall amid broader economic headwinds

    Japan Prime Minister Ishiba to step down

  • BoJ minutes reveal ongoing caution on economy amid inflation & market risks
  • Japan's manufacturing PMI revised upward to 48.7 in April
  • Japan to spend ¥388 billion on relief measures for U.S. tariff impact

data.title: Stock index futures rise as markets continue to shrug off shutdown concerns
data.description:
Stock index futures were higher on Friday, as Wall Street is hoping to remain unfazed by the U.S. government shutdown.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) +0.4%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) +0.4% and Dow futures (INDU) +0.3%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 1 basis point to 4.10%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 1 basis point to 3.56%.

Wall Street climbed higher on Thursday as both the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite and benchmark S&P 500 hit new intraday record highs as the government shutdown hit a second day.

"In terms of the shutdown, yesterday brought no sign of an end to the impasse, with investors becoming increasingly concerned that it could drag on into next week," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said. 

The shutdown came into effect at midnight on Wednesday after Republicans and Democrats failed to reach a federal funding deal. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on Thursday told CNBC that U.S. GDP growth could take a hit from the shutdown.

Wall Street has largely shrugged off the shutdown. On the prediction market Kalshi, the odds of the length of the shutdown lasting more than 10 days ticked up to 69%. 

Due to the closure, market participants will be without the September nonfarm payrolls report. The data was widely seen as a key report for the Federal Reserve to assess the labor market situation. 

"Indeed, we’d normally be previewing the payroll numbers this morning, but the shutdown has seen that postponed, along with the usual weekly jobless claims yesterday," Reid added. 

Other economic data, like the September PMI Composite Final and the September ISM Services Index, will land during market hours. The former is expected to come in at 53.6 and at 51.6 for the latter.

data.title: European indexes extend gains, touching fresh highs
data.description:
London (UKX) +0.41% to 9,463, heading for a record close with nearly 2% weekly gains.

Germany (DAX:IND) +0.23% to 24,475,  its highest level since July 9 and marking the sixth straight session of gains. Germany service activity at 8-month high.

France (CAC:IND) +0.36% to 8,085, its highest since late March, lifted by gains in bank shares. France service activity shrinks in September. Industrial production in France fell 0.7% M/M in August.

In other parts of Europe, Italy Services PMI rose to 52.5 in September 2025 from 51.5 in August.

Spain services sector growth accelerates in September.

Euro Area services growth ticks higher, but still muted.

The Services PMI in Sweden climbed to 57.7 in September.

The number of people registered as out of work in Norway rose to a seasonally adjusted 64.4Kin September 2025.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) turned 0.44% higher to 570, touching fresh records driven by strength in basic resources and banking shares, while ongoing optimism around AI continued to support global sentiment. 

The euro traded just above $1.17, staying close to last month’s four-year high of $1.192, as markets weighed the widening policy gap between the European Central Bank and the Federal Reserve.

Coming up in the session: UK services PMI expected shortly.

In the bond market, the yield on the US 10-year Treasury was unchanged at 4.09%.

Germany's 10-year yield was unchanged at 2.70%.

UK's 10-year yield was down 2 basis points to 4.70%. 

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)
